note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration (s06/s10 July 11 re-render), in spoken order, split at the script's break tags and sentence boundaries into caption-sized pieces. Caption text is never rewritten, summarized, or reordered. Block windows are the measured onsets of each rendered block inside the joined master (normalized cross-correlation of each WC-02-04-sNN.mp3 against WC-02-04-full-narration-v1.0.mp3, all match scores >= 0.9997); the theoretical audio+0.4 tail model ends at 152.548s but the ffprobe master duration is 152.111s (the joined master uses ~0.36s inter-block gaps), so measured onsets are used for accurate phrase placement.

phrases.text: Every prepaid card the network has sold lives on one screen.
This is the gift card portfolio.
Four headline tiles up top, three insight panels, and a searchable card list below.
On a support call, this is almost always where you start.
Four tiles run across the top.
How many cards exist, how much is still owed on them,
how many are live, and how many are spent to zero.
Read them left to right for the whole portfolio at a glance.
First panel, issuance over the last year.
The line climbs, up about nine percent, with the running total in the corner.
It tells you the program is growing before you open a report.
Next to it, where the money sits.
Most cards carry small amounts; only a thin slice hold a hundred dollars or more.
So a big disputed balance is already the unusual case.
The third panel is portfolio health.
Most active, a good share redeemed, a few expired.
The same split as the status column, rolled into one bar.
Now the list itself.
One number sits under the title: the record count,
a hundred sixteen thousand cards and change, the same figure as the total tile up top.
Two buttons sit above the table.
Delete removes a card.
Issue Gift Card creates one.
Both write to real records, so we point and read, and never click either.
Under the buttons, one search box.
Type a card number, a customer, a location, or a status, and the list narrows to the match.
When a caller has the card in hand, this is the fast path.
Six columns describe each card.
The number, who owns it, where it sold,
what is left, whether it is still good, and when it last moved.
Balance, status, last used carry most calls.
Now the rows themselves.
They load live, twenty-five cards to a page, thousands of pages deep.
Where the customer cell shows a dash, no one is linked to that card.
And if the table ever says it couldn't load, refresh before you quote a balance.
Here is the everyday move.
A customer reads you their card number.
You drop it into the search,
and the row returns the balance, the status, and the last-used date.
Read those back and the call is answered.
So, the gift card screen.
Totals up top, three quick-read panels, and a list where every lookup lands.
Find the card by number, read its balance and status,
and you have handled almost every gift card call.
